steps.prompt: Which word is the imperative verb?

hints.diem: 'diem' is a noun — the direct object meaning 'day'

hints.Indicative: Indicative present would be 'carpis' (2nd person) — 'Carpe' is the imperative singular
hints.Subjunctive: Subjunctive would be 'carpas' — 'Carpe' is the imperative form
hints.Infinitive: Infinitive would be 'carpere' — 'Carpe' is imperative singular

hints.capio, capere, cepi, captum: These are the principal parts of 'capere' (to seize/take) — similar meaning but different verb
hints.cano, canere, cecini, cantum: These are the principal parts of 'canere' (to sing)
hints.caedo, caedere, cecidi, caesum: These are the principal parts of 'caedere' (to cut/kill)

hints.Nominative: Nominative of 'dies' is 'dies' — 'diem' with -em ending is accusative

hints.1st: 1st declension nouns end in -a — 'dies' with genitive 'diei' is 5th declension
hints.3rd: 3rd declension nouns have genitive -is — 'dies' with genitive 'diei' is 5th declension
hints.4th: 4th declension has genitive -us — 'dies' with genitive 'diei' is 5th declension

hints.diem: 'diem' is accusative — 'credula' is nominative feminine, modifying the addressee
hints.postero: 'postero' is dative/ablative masculine/neuter — 'credula' is nominative feminine
hints.minimum: 'minimum' is an adverb here — 'credula' is an adjective modifying the implied 'you'

hints.Nominative: Nominative would be 'posterus' — 'postero' with -o ending is dative or ablative
hints.Ablative: Ablative is possible, but 'credula' governs the dative — 'trusting in the next day'
hints.Accusative: Accusative would be 'posterum' — 'postero' is dative here

translation: Seize the day, trusting as little as possible in the next
